title: System-Level Design of Scalable Fluxonium Quantum Processors with Double-Transmon Couplers
authors: Guo Xuan Chan, Wangwei Lan, Tenghui Wang, Xizheng Ma, Chunqing Deng, Lijing Jin
abstract: Fluxonium qubits combine long coherence times with strong anharmonicity, making them a promising platform for scalable superconducting quantum processors. Recent experiments have demonstrated high-fidelity operations in multi-qubit processors while suppressing stray qubit interactions using fluxonium-transmon-fluxonium (FTF) architectures. However, scaling such systems to larger arrays is constrained by a trade-off between achievable coupling strength, crosstalk suppression and qubit-qubit spacing required for wiring in a two-dimensional architecture. Multimode couplers, such as the double-transmon coupler (DTC), provide a promising pathway to overcome this limitation by enabling stronger interactions without compromising qubit spacing and isolation. Here, we develop a quantitative design framework for fluxonium-based quantum processors employing DTCs. Central to this work is a frequency-partitioned architecture that places qubit transitions, tunable-coupler excitations, and resonator modes in well-separated spectral regions. This structured allocation reduces parameter interdependence and enables the concurrent optimization of gate operations, readout, and qubit reset. By formulating device design as a multi-objective optimization problem under realistic experimental constraints and fabrication-induced disorder, we develop a tractable sequential workflow and determine a feasible parameter regime that simultaneously supports high-fidelity single- and two-qubit gates, fast qubit reset, and robust dispersive readout. These results establish a system-level architectural methodology that links circuit parameters to processor-level performance, and provide an experimentally actionable pathway toward scalable fluxonium quantum processors.
summary: This paper presents a system-level design strategy for scaling fluxonium quantum processors. By using double-transmon couplers, the authors propose a way to achieve strong qubit interactions without increasing crosstalk or requiring excessive physical spacing. They introduce a frequency-partitioned architecture that separates qubit, coupler, and resonator modes to optimize gate performance and readout. The resulting framework provides a practical roadmap for building larger, high-fidelity superconducting quantum arrays.
main_problem: Scaling fluxonium-based quantum processors is limited by a trade-off between coupling strength, crosstalk suppression, and the physical spacing required for wiring in 2D architectures.
main_result: The authors develop a quantitative design framework and a frequency-partitioned architecture using double-transmon couplers (DTC) that enables high-fidelity gates, fast reset, and robust readout while maintaining isolation.
method: The study employs a multi-objective optimization approach under realistic experimental constraints and fabrication-induced disorder, utilizing a sequential design workflow.
model_or_system: A scalable superconducting quantum processor architecture based on fluxonium qubits coupled via multi-mode double-transmon couplers (DTC) and integrated with resonators.
key_observables: Single- and two-qubit gate fidelities, qubit reset speed, and dispersive readout robustness.
important_parameters: Frequency-partitioned spectral regions for qubit transitions, coupler excitations, and resonator modes; coupling strengths; and fabrication-induced disorder.
main_assumptions: The design assumes realistic experimental constraints and accounts for fabrication-induced disorder in the optimization process.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the scaling challenge, proposes a DTC-based architecture, details a frequency-partitioned design framework, applies multi-objective optimization, and validates the resulting parameter regime for processor-level performance.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant for researchers in quantum optics and open quantum systems as it addresses the engineering of complex, multi-mode light-matter interfaces and the management of spectral crowding in large-scale circuit QED architectures.
analysis_basis: abstract only; PDF text extraction failed or PDF unavailable
